column1:
The waveform from an abdominal vessel displays what two characteristics?

A: flow reversal during diastole
B: mild phasicity and pulsatility
C: triphasic flow and no respiratory variation
D: flow reversal during augmentation
column2:
Answer: B. mild phasicity and pulsatility

Feedback:
The waveform displays mild respiratory variation (phasicity) and pulsatility in a normal renal vein. Please note that the definition
of pulsatility indicates regular variations in flow. Not all pulsatility in venous flow is a result of the ripple effects of cardiac
contraction. These findings are considered normal in renal vessels. If venous congestion occurs, the flow will become
increasingly pulsatile and flow reversal can develop during atrial contraction.

column1:
The normal flow profile within the aorta is termed:

A: Phasic flow
B: Plug flow
C: Recoil flow
D: Continuous flow
column2:
Answer: B. Plug flow

Feedback:
The layers of blood flow in the aorta move at relatively the same speed and this is called plug flow. Laminar flow patterns occur
in smaller vessels. The velocity of the layers of blood flow in these vessels vary slightly by decreasing as they near the vessel
wall.

column1:
The most cephalic branch of the abdominal aorta is:

A: SMA
B: celiac axis
C: Splenic artery
D: Common hepatic artery
column2:
Answer: B. celiac axis

Feedback:
The celiac axis is the first abdominal branch of the aorta followed by the origin of the SMA approximately 2cm more distal on
the aorta. The renal arteries are the next distal branch followed by the IMA.

column1:
Which of the following factors are related to potential hemodynamic changes within a vessel?

A: vessel length and blood viscosity, but not vessel radius
B: blood viscosity and vessel radius, but not vessel length
C: vessel length and vessel radius, but not blood viscosity
D: vessel length, vessel radius and blood viscosity
column2:
Answer: D. vessel length, vessel radius and blood viscosity

Feedback:
Changes in vessel length, vessel radius and blood viscosity are related to potential hemodynamic changes within a vessel. Know
Poiseuille's Law!

column1:
According to the attached color map, what color will the blood be demonstrated that is moving toward the probe at an average velocity of 100cm/s?

A: red
B: blue
C: white/light blue
D: yellow
column2:
Answer: C. white/light blue

Feedback:
The speed of the blood exceeds the scale displayed therefore aliasing of the color display will occur. Normally flow toward the
probe would be red, IF the average velocity is less than 0.43 m/s. Flow moving at 1.0 m/s (100cm/s) exceeds the top limit of 0.43
m/s which will cause aliasing. The white or light blue color of the blue color bar will be seen and is related to the "wrap around"
of the color display.

column1:
Which imaging plane can produce a longitudinal image of the IVC and the aorta on the same image?

A: mid sagittal
B: coronal
C: coronal and transverse
D: transverse
column2:
Answer: B. coronal

Feedback:
Placing the patient in the decubitus position and placing the probe on the lateral aspect of the abdomen will demonstrate a
longitudinal view of the aorta and IVC side by side on the image. A transverse view can also demonstrate both great vessels on
the same image but will not provide a longitudinal view.

column1:
Which of the following terms can be used to describe the normal Doppler waveform in the proximal renal vein?

A: continuous flow
B: respiratory phasicity and mild pulsatility
C: mild pulsatility with no respiratory changes
D: respiratory phasicity with no pulsatility
column2:
Answer: B. respiratory phasicity and mild pulsatility

Feedback:
Venous flow in the IVC, hepatic and proximal renal veins normally demonstrate respiratory phasicity and mild cardiac pulsatility.
At the renal hilum, the flow is more continuous with minimal respiratory phasicity.
Image Credit: D'Andrea A, Del Giudice C, Fabiani D, Caputo A, Sabatella F, Cante L, Palermi S, Desiderio A, Tagliamonte E,
Liccardo B, et al. The Incremental Role of Multiorgan Point-of-Care Ultrasounds in the Emergency Setting. International Journal of
Environmental Research and Public Health. 2023; 20(3):2088. https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph20032088

column1:
Letter T indicates which of the following structures?

A: superior mesenteric artery
B: IVC
C: left renal artery
D: right renal artery
column2:
Answer: D. right renal artery

Feedback:

column1:
Poiseuille's Law states that the ___________________ has the most significant effect on the blood flow in a vessel.

A: vessel length
B: fluid viscosity
C: vessel radius
D: pressure gradient
column2:
Answer: C. vessel radius

Feedback:
Poiseuille's Law demonstrates the relationship between pressure, flow volume and resistance. A small change in vessel radius
causes a dramatic change in the blood flow within. Note the equation lists the radius of the blood vessel and it is raised to the
4th power. This is a significant numerical increase (or decrease) with changes to the radius measurement.
Tip for these types of questions: Know your equations! memorizing the Bernoulli, Poiseuille's Law, and Doppler equation will be
very helpful in answering relationship questions. Characteristics on the top of the division equation are directly related to the
outcome number. Characteristics on the bottom of a division equation are indirectly related to the outcome number.
For example: Flow volume = Pressure divided by Resistance. Q= P/R
As pressure increases, flow volume increases (direct relationship).
As resistance increases, flow volume decreases (indirect or inverse relationship).

column1:
Which of the following statements about the IVC is correct?

A: The IVC empties blood into the left atrium.
B: The IVC is the most posterior vessel in the abdomen.
C: Congestive heart failure and pulmonary HTN can cause IVC dilatation.
D: The IVC should normally maintain a relatively constant diameter with respiration.
column2:
Answer: C. Congestive heart failure and pulmonary HTN can cause IVC dilatation.

Feedback:
The IVC courses anterior to the level of the aorta and empties blood into the right atrium. The IVC should normally change
diameter with respiration. The aorta maintains a constant diameter with respiration. Congestive heart failure , significant
tricuspid regurgitation and pulmonary HTN can cause IVC dilatation.

column1:
A mesenteric to aortic ratio is considered normal when it is ________________.

A: >1.0
B: 1.0 or less
C: >3.0
D: <3.0
column2:
Answer: B. 1.0 or less

Feedback:
The mesenteric velocity should be similar to the aortic velocity, which would yield a ratio of around 1.0. As the stenosis in the
SMA increases, the velocity increases and the ratio increases. SMA/aorta ratio >3.0 indicates significant stenosis.

column1:
Letter K indicates which of the following vessels?

A: middle hepatic vein
B: left portal vein
C: left hepatic vein
D: right portal vein
column2:
Answer: C. left hepatic vein

Feedback:
I. right hepatic vein
J. middle hepatic vein
K. left hepatic vein

column1:
The hepatic artery carries _______ of the blood entering the liver.

A: 20-30%
B: 50-60%
C: 80-90%
D: 100%
column2:
Answer: A. 20-30%

Feedback:
The portal vein carries 70-80% of the blood entering the liver, but it is lower in oxygen saturation at 85%. The hepatic artery
carries 20-30% of the blood into the liver and it has a 95% oxygen saturation. The portal venous system supplies just over half of
the oxygen to the liver tissue because of the high volume of flow. The hepatic artery supplies just under half of the oxygen to
the liver tissues.

column1:
What is the most common normal variant in renal artery anatomy?

A: right renal artery courses anterior to the IVC
B: absence of the right renal artery
C: absence of the left renal artery
D: duplicated renal arteries
column2:
Answer: D. duplicated renal arteries

Feedback:
1/3 patients have duplicated renal artery (most common anatomic variant).

column1:
Which of the following describes the best technique used to visualize and locate the inferior mesenteric artery?

A: transverse approach, locate renal arteries and slide inferiorly
B: sagittal approach to the right of midline near umbilicus
C: coronal approach on the left side mid flank
D: sagittal approach to the left of midline near umbilicus
column2:
Answer: A. transverse approach, locate renal arteries and slide inferiorly

Feedback:
The best technique used to visualize and locate the inferior mesenteric artery is to use a transverse approach to evaluate the
aorta. After locating the renal artery origins, slide inferiorly to look for the IMA. The IMA originates from the anterior aorta just
distal to the renal artery origins.

column1:
As pressure increases, resistance must ______________________ to maintain constant flow volume.

A: stay constant
B: resistance does not vary related to pressure and flow volume
C: decrease
D: increase
column2:
Answer: D. increase

Feedback:
As pressure increases, flow will increase if there is no change in resistance. In order to maintain a constant flow, when pressure
increases, resistance must increase.

column1:
Which of the following terms can be used to describe the normal Doppler waveform in the portal vein?

A: blunted with respiratory variation
B: moderately pulsatile flow due to the vessel proximity to the heart
C: prominent respiratory variation and mild pulsatility
D: mild pulsatility with respiratory variation
column2:
Answer: D. mild pulsatility with respiratory variation

Feedback:
Venous flow in the portal system normally demonstrates mild respiratory variation and pulsatility. The increased thickness of
the vessel walls and location in the abdomen prevent significant cardiac effects on the flow pattern. Mildly pulsatile flow is
considered normal with a pulsatility index less than 30%. The pulsatility is not considered "cardiac pulsatility" as the variation is
due to the normal flow reversal from the hepatic veins into the venous sinusoids that slows portal inflow. As hepatic vein flow
reversal increases with congestion, the pulsatility of the portal vein will increase to abnormal levels.

column1: Use your mouse to place your cursor over the hepatic artery and click to mark the vessel. If the vessel is not demonstrated on the image, mark the purple box that says "Not demonstrated on the image".
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
This image demonstrates the aorta, SMA and the left renal vein. The celiac axis and its branches are cephalad to the level of this
image. The SMA is visible anterior to the aorta, therefore the hepatic artery would not be visualized in this plane.

column1:
The _________________ is formed by the junction of the splenic vein and the superior mesenteric vein.

A: main portal vein
B: inferior mesenteric vein
C: left hepatic vein
D: right hepatic vein
column2:
Answer: A. main portal vein

Feedback:
The MPV is formed by the junction of the splenic vein and the superior mesenteric vein.

column1:
Which of the following vessels does not drain into the IVC?

A: right gonadal vein
B: right renal vein
C: left hepatic vein
D: splenic vein
column2:
Answer: D. splenic vein

Feedback:
The left gonadal vein empties into the left renal vein while the right gonadal vein empties directly into the IVC. The splenic vein is
part of the portal system.

column1:
Blood within the pulmonary veins:

A: contains a high oxygen content and flows into the right atrium
B: contains a low oxygen content and flows into the left atrium
C: contains high oxygen content and flows into the left atrium
D: is normally high resistance
column2:
Answer: C. contains high oxygen content and flows into the left atrium

Feedback:
Blood within the pulmonary veins contains high oxygen content and flows into the left atrium. These veins are the only veins in
the body with a high oxygen content as they carry blood from the lungs to the heart. Abdominal and extremity veins are
typically lower in oxygen content than the arterial circulatory system.

column1:
Which two cursors would be used to measure the acceleration time for the flow in this renal artery tracing?

A: red and blue
B: blue and green
C: green and red
D: green and yellow
column2:
Answer: C. green and red

Feedback:
The AT is measured by placing a cursor at the start of the systolic upstroke and at the early systolic peak (if present). The
velocities used to calculate the RI are measured by placing the cursor at the true peak systolic velocity and the end diastolic
velocity.

column1:
The primary mechanisms that control arterial flow volume changes during the cardiac cycle are:

A: viscosity, vessel radius
B: vessel length, peripheral resistance
C: cardiac output, viscosity
D: cardiac output, peripheral resistance
column2:
Answer: D. cardiac output, peripheral resistance

Feedback:
The primary mechanisms that control arterial flow volume changes during the cardiac cycle are cardiac output and peripheral
resistance.

column1: Use your mouse to place your cursor over the superior mesenteric artery and click to mark the vessel. If the vessel is not demonstrated on the image, mark the purple box that says "Not demonstrated on the image".
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The SMA is identified anterior to the aorta and posterior to the body of the pancreas. The left renal vein courses between the
aorta and the SMA to join the IVC.

column1: Use your mouse to place your cursor over the splenic artery and click to mark the vessel. If the vessel is not demonstrated on the image, mark the purple box that says "Not demonstrated on the image".
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The splenic artery is not visible in this imaging plane. The splenic vein is demonstrated posterior to the body of the pancreas.

column1:
Letter I indicates which of the following vessels?

A: right hepatic vein
B: left hepatic vein
C: right portal vein
D: left portal vein
column2:
Answer: A. right hepatic vein

Feedback:
I. right hepatic vein
J. middle hepatic vein
K. left hepatic vein

column1: Use your mouse to place your cursor over the left renal artery and click to mark the vessel.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The image is called the banana peel view. The aorta is the banana and the renal arteries are "peeling" off the sides of the aorta.
The image is obtained with an oblique or coronal view of the abdomen. The anterior image is the right side of the abdomen with
the liver and IVC demonstrated closest to the transducer. The right renal artery is the branch of the aorta coursing toward the
right side and the transducer (red). The left renal artery is the branch of the aorta coursing away from the right side and the
transducer (blue).

column1: Use your mouse to place your cursor over the inferior vena cava and click to mark the vessel. If the vessel is not demonstrated on the image, mark the purple box that says "Not demonstrated on the image".
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The IVC courses posterior to the head of the pancreas.

column1:
The _____________________ supplies arterial blood to the small intestine and proximal colon, while the __________________ supplies blood to the distal colon.

A: left gastric artery, inferior mesenteric arteries
B: celiac axis, superior mesenteric arteries
C: gastroduodenal artery, superior mesenteric arteries
D: superior and inferior mesenteric arteries
column2:
Answer: D. superior and inferior mesenteric arteries

Feedback:
The proximal colon and small intestine are supplied by the SMA. The distal colon is supplied by the IMA.

column1:
Which gonadal vein empties directly into the IVC?

A: Right gonadal vein
B: Left gonadal vein
C: Right and left gonadal veins
D: Neither gonadal vein empties directly into the IVC
column2:
Answer: A. Right gonadal vein

Feedback:
The right gonadal vein empties into the IVC, while the left gonadal vein empties into the left renal vein.

column1:
The diameter of a normal portal vein should not exceed:

A: 10mm
B: 11mm
C: 12mm
D: 13mm
column2:
Answer: D. 13mm

Feedback:
The diameter of the portal vein should not exceed 13mm.

column1:
Which of the following describes a cardiovascular application of the Bernoulli Principle?

A: explains the relationship between pressure, resistance and flow volume
B: explains the relationship of vessel radius and flow volume
C: explains the turbulence and layers of flow separation within the carotid bulb
D: explains the reason for aliasing of the Doppler signal at high velocities
column2:
Answer: C. explains the turbulence and layers of flow separation within the carotid bulb

Feedback:
Bernoulli Principle explains the turbulence and layers of flow separation within the carotid bulb. When there is high velocity
flow, there is low pressure and vice versa. The Bernoulli equation describes relationship between area, velocity, and pressure at
a stenosis or widening of a vessel.
Poiseuille's Law explains the changes in resistance and pressure that are related to flow volume. It also describes the effects of
vessel radius and length on flow volume

column1:
Normally, what changes occur in the waveform of the SMA postprandially?

A: resistance decreases
B: resistance increases
C: diastolic flow decreases
D: resistance and diastolic flow decreases
column2:
Answer: A. resistance decreases

Feedback:
When digestion starts, the digestive organs require an increase in blood flow. The resistance in the SMA blood flow will decrease
and diastolic flow will increase. When distal resistance to flow decreases, more blood flow occurs in diastole. If stenosis is
present, there will be an increase in systolic velocity (>2.75m/s)with no change in the resistance to the flow.

column1: Use your mouse to place your cursor over the right renal vein and click to mark the vessel. If the vessel is not demonstrated on the image, mark the purple box that says "Not demonstrated on the image".
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The SMA is identified anterior to the aorta and posterior to the body of the pancreas. The left renal vein courses between the
aorta and the SMA to join the IVC. The right renal vein is much shorter than the left and joins the right lateral aspect of the IVC.

column1:
A common anatomic variant of the mesenteric arterial anatomy is:

A: The SMA and IMA share a common trunk
B: the celiac artery is absent and the liver, stomach and spleen are supplied with blood from the inferior mesenteric artery
C: the celiac artery and SMA originate from the posterior aorta
D: the celiac axis and SMA share a common trunk
column2:
Answer: D. the celiac axis and SMA share a common trunk

Feedback:
A common anatomic variant of the mesenteric arterial anatomy is the celiac axis and SMA share a common trunk. Other known
variants include the hepatic artery originates from the SMA or the hepatic artery originates directly from the aorta.

column1: Use your mouse to place your cursor over the IVC and click to mark the structure.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The IVC is posterior to the caudate lobe of the liver.

column1:
Bradycardia is defined as a heart rate less than ________bpm.

A: 45
B: 50
C: 60
D: 65
column2:
Answer: C. 60

Feedback:
Bradycardia is defined as a heart rate less than 60 bpm. Tachycardia is defined as a heart rate more than 100 bpm.

column1:
Which of the following is the best anatomic landmark used to locate the left renal artery?

A: origin of the celiac axis
B: left renal vein
C: iliac bifurcation
D: portal confluence
column2:
Answer: B. left renal vein

Feedback:
The left renal vein courses anterior to the aorta and posterior to the SMA to reach the IVC. The LRA can normally be identified
immediately posterior to the left renal vein in the transverse view of the abdomen.

column1:
Which abdominal vessel arises from the anterior surface of the aorta approximately 2 cm distal to the celiac axis?

A: inferior mesenteric artery
B: superior mesenteric artery
C: common hepatic artery
D: gastroduodenal artery
column2:
Answer: B. superior mesenteric artery

Feedback:
The SMA arises from the anterior surface of the aorta approximately 2 cm distal to the celiac axis.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes this image?

A: The image is incorrectly labelled and the diameter measurement is performed incorrectly.
B: The image is incorrectly labelled but the diameter measurement is performed correctly.
C: The image is correctly labelled and the diameter measurement is performed correctly.
D: The image is correctly labelled but the diameter measurement is performed incorrectly.
column2:
Answer: A. The image is incorrectly labelled and the diameter measurement is performed incorrectly.

Feedback:
The image is incorrectly labelled and the diameter measurement is performed incorrectly. The measurement is being obtained
above the celiac and SMA origin. This indicates the proximal aorta segment. The aorta should be measured from outer wall to
outer wall for an accurate diameter. This image demonstrates the outer wall to inner wall diameter.

column1:
Normal hepatic venous flow will demonstrate:

A: two large antegrade diastolic and systolic waves followed by a small retrograde component that corresponds with the atrial contraction
B: two large retrograde diastolic and systolic waves followed by a small antegrade component that corresponds with the atrial contraction
C: two large retrograde diastolic and systolic waves followed by a small retrograde component that corresponds with the atrial contraction
D: two large antegrade diastolic and systolic waves followed by a small antegrade component that corresponds with the atrial contraction
column2:
Answer: A. two large antegrade diastolic and systolic waves followed by a small retrograde component that corresponds with the atrial contraction

Feedback:
Normal HV flow is considered triphasic. The tracing normally demonstrates respiratory phasicity and cardiac pulsatility. The
majority of flow moves forward into the right atrium until the atrial "kick" occurs, The Doppler tracing will demonstrate two large
antegrade systolic (S wave) and diastolic (D wave) waves followed by a small retrograde component (A wave) that corresponds
with the atrial contraction.

column1:
Which of the following is directly related to the calculated peak Doppler velocity in an artery?

A: sine of the cursor angle
B: vessel radius
C: flow volume
D: Doppler shift frequency
column2:
Answer: D. Doppler shift frequency

Feedback:
The Doppler shift frequency is directly related to the calculated Doppler velocity.

column1:
The preferred imaging plane to measure the AP dimension of the aorta is:

A: coronal
B: transverse
C: sagittal
D: radial
column2:
Answer: C. sagittal

Feedback:
The sagittal plane is the preferred imaging plane to measure the AP dimension of the aorta.

column1:
Which of the following statements can be used to describe a normal renal artery waveform?

A: low resistance with increased diastolic flow
B: increased diastolic flow reversal
C: biphasic with mild diastolic flow reversal
D: pulsus alternans
column2:
Answer: A. low resistance with increased diastolic flow

Feedback:
Blood flow into organs is normally continuous throughout the cardiac cycle. The flow is described as low resistance or
monophasic and demonstrates increased antegrade flow in diastole. Pulsus alternans is the methodical variation in peak
systolic velocity on every other beat.

column1:
Which of the following flow characteristics is/are seen in a normal hepatic vein Doppler tracing?

A: cardiac pulsatility only
B: respiratory phasicity only
C: monophasic flow and respiratory phasicity
D: cardiac pulsatility and respiratory phasicity
column2:
Answer: D. cardiac pulsatility and respiratory phasicity

Feedback:
Cardiac pulsatility is normal seen in the hepatic veins and the upper abdominal portion IVC. The motion of the heart causes
vibrations within the chest that are carried throughout the adjacent structures. The flexibility of the IVC and hepatic veins allow
the vibrations to affect the flow inside causing pulsatility.
As the diaphragm moves up and down with respiration, the intra-abdominal pressure is affected. Inspiration causes the
diaphragm to move down which increases the intra-abdominal pressure. This causes flow in the abdomen and lower
extremities to decrease or cease movement. The diaphragm moves upward with expiration and the intra-abdominal pressure
decreases allowing flow to move toward the heart again.

column1: Use your mouse to place your cursor over the left renal vein and click to mark the vessel. If the vessel is not demonstrated on the image, mark the purple box that says "Not demonstrated on the image".
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
This image demonstrates the aorta, SMA and the left renal vein. Be careful not to mistake the distal renal vein with the renal
artery. Note the red color indicates flow toward the transducer, which would be normal for the left renal vein in this view. The
renal artery would demonstrate flow moving in the opposite direction (blue)

column1:
In cases of interrupted IVC, how does the blood from the distal IVC and lower extremities reach the right atrium?

A: an AV malformation of the pelvis must be present for the patient to survive after birth
B: iliac veins empty directly into the renal veins
C: umbilical vein serves as a collateral pathway and empties into the portal system
D: azygos vein serves as a collateral pathway and empties into the SVC
column2:
Answer: D. azygos vein serves as a collateral pathway and empties into the SVC

Feedback:
Interrupted IVC: the intrahepatic segment of the IVC does not develop; the azygos vein drains the flow from the pelvis and lower
extremities into the SVC; hepatic veins empty directly into the right atrium

column1:
Which of the following will increase spectral broadening on the image?

A: increased sample volume size
B: increased PRF
C: more laminar flow
D: increased wall filter
column2:
Answer: A. increased sample volume size

Feedback:
Increasing sample volume size on PW Doppler will allow for sampling of more blood cells moving at different velocities. The
increase in the number of velocities displayed will cause fill in of the spectral window.

column1:
Normal flow in the splenic artery:

A: is laminar.
B: is hepatopetal.
C: demonstrates Reynold's number >2000.
D: is high resistant.
column2:
Answer: C. demonstrates Reynold's number >2000.

Feedback:
The splenic artery is the most tortuous artery in the body and carries very turbulent flow. A Reynolds number over 2000
indicates the point where turbulence is identified on color Doppler.

column1:
What is the proper patient position and respiration for assessing the portal vein diameter?

A: supine with quiet respiration
B: left lateral decubitus position with deep inspiration
C: supine with inspiration and the Valsalva strain
D: right lateral decubitus position with deep inspiration
column2:
Answer: A. supine with quiet respiration

Feedback:
The portal vein should be assessed with the patient supine and with quiet respiration. The measurement should be obtained at
the point the portal vein crosses the IVC.

column1:
Resistance to flow ______________ as blood travels distally through the abdominal aorta.

A: decreases by 50%
B: decreases slightly
C: remains constant
D: increases
column2:
Answer: D. increases

Feedback:
The proximal portion of the aorta demonstrates lower resistance, when compared with the distal portion. The branches of the
upper and mid aorta feed low resistance capillary beds in the organs which affects the aortic flow causing it to be biphasic. The
distal aorta branches into vessels that supply muscles and supporting structures of the legs. These tissues provide a much
higher resistance to flow than the abdominal organs, causing triphasic flow in the distal aorta.

column1:
Where is the arc of Riolan found?

A: within the mesentery
B: within the brain
C: at the popliteal fossa
D: within the kidney
column2:
Answer: A. within the mesentery

Feedback:
The arc of Riolan and the marginal artery of Drummond connect the SMA and IMA through the mesentery. They offer potential
for collateral flow if a mesenteric obstruction occurs.

column1:
Where is the marginal artery of Drummond located?

A: connects the SMA and IMA through the mesentery
B: at the splenic hilum
C: connect the dorsalis pedis artery to the posterior tibial artery in the foot
D: part of the cerebrovascular circulatory system
column2:
Answer: A. connects the SMA and IMA through the mesentery

Feedback:
The marginal artery of Drummond connects the SMA and IMA through the mesentery and is a possible pathway for collateral
flow.

column1:
The release of norepinephrine into the blood steam has what effect on the arterioles?

A: vasoconstriction
B: stenosis
C: vasodilation
D: decreased flow resistance
column2:
Answer: A. vasoconstriction

Feedback:
Norepinephrine causes vasoconstriction and adrenaline causes vasodilation.

column1:
The seagull sign refers to which of the following vessels?

A: celiac axis
B: three hepatic veins
C: common femoral bifurcation
D: bilateral renal origins
column2:
Answer: A. celiac axis

Feedback:
The celiac axis and the two branches visible on US form the seagull sign. The CHA and Splenic artery form the wings of the bird.

column1:
Which imaging plane can demonstrate the IVC and the aorta on the same image in a normal patient?

A: coronal only
B: coronal and transverse
C: mid sagittal only
D: transverse only
column2:
Answer: B. coronal and transverse

Feedback:

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes normal flow in the hepatic vessels?

A: the hepatic artery demonstrates hepatopetal flow
B: the hepatic artery demonstrates hepatofugal flow
C: the portal veins demonstrate hepatofugal flow
D: the hepatic veins demonstrate hepatopetal flow
column2:
Answer: A. the hepatic artery demonstrates hepatopetal flow

Feedback:
Hepatofugal flow is flow that is moving out of the liver. Hepatopetal flow is flow that is moving toward/into the liver. The normal
HA and PV demonstrate hepatopetal flow. The hepatic veins demonstrate hepatofugal flow.

column1:
Where should the Doppler sample be placed to obtain the highest velocity when evaluating laminar flow?

A: adjacent to the anterior wall
B: varies with the type of vessel evaluated
C: in the center of the vessel
D: adjacent to the posterior wall
column2:
Answer: C. in the center of the vessel

Feedback:
The Doppler sample should be placed in the center of the vessel to obtain the highest velocity when evaluating laminar flow.
The red blood cells (RBCs) in the center of the vessel are moving at relatively the same speed with laminar flow. This results in a
waveform with a prominent spectral window. The closer the cursor gets to the wall of the vessel, the greater the friction effect
of the RBCs against the wall. This friction causes variation in velocity and increased spectral broadening.

column1:
Which blood vessel courses anterior to the aorta and posterior to the SMA?

A: inferior mesenteric vein
B: right renal vein
C: left renal vein
D: superior mesenteric vein
column2:
Answer: C. left renal vein

Feedback:
The left renal vein courses from the left kidney across the abdomen between the aorta and SMA to reach the IVC.

column1:
What is the proper caliper placement for assessing the portal vein diameter?

A: outer wall to outer wall at the junction of the splenic vein and portal vein
B: outer wall to outer wall at the point the portal vein crosses the IVC
C: inner wall to inner wall at the point the portal vein crosses the IVC
D: inner wall to inner wall at the junction of the splenic vein and portal vein
column2:
Answer: C. inner wall to inner wall at the point the portal vein crosses the IVC

Feedback:
The portal vein should be assessed with the patient supine and with quiet respiration. The measurement should be obtained at
the point the portal vein crosses the IVC.

column1:
Which of the following normally demonstrates a low resistance Doppler flow profile?

A: external carotid artery
B: hepatic artery and renal artery
C: renal artery and internal iliac artery
D: external carotid artery, renal and hepatic arteries
column2:
Answer: B. hepatic artery and renal artery

Feedback:
All vessels that feed organs will have low resistance vascular beds which lead to a low resistance waveform. "Organ flow" will
typically be low resistance and constant flow is needed for the organs to function. The renal and hepatic arteries will have low
resistance flow with increased diastolic flow because they constantly function. "Extremity flow" is typically high resistance as
muscles/tissue need much less inflow, especially at rest. The external carotid artery and its branches feed mainly muscles and
tissue, therefore the flow demonstrates a higher resistance waveform.

column1:
The attached image is taken from the celiac axis on an abdominal Doppler exam. Which of the following is the most likely finding in this patient when the vessel is re-evaluated after eating?

A: no change in flow pattern
B: decreased resistance and increased peak velocity
C: increased resistance and increased peak velocity
D: decreased resistance with no change in peak velocity
column2:
Answer: A. no change in flow pattern

Feedback:
The celiac artery waveform is usually unchanged after eating in the normal patient. The CA mainly supplies the liver and spleen
with blood and these organs are low resistance vascular beds. The fasting waveform and peak velocity on this patient are well
within normal limits which indicates a normal celiac axis.

column1:
The term used to describe the thickness of blood is:

A: impedance
B: vasoconstriction
C: viscosity
D: resistance
column2:
Answer: C. viscosity

Feedback:
The higher the viscosity the thicker the fluid. Oil and honey have a higher viscosity than water.
